title: The Dismissal That May Have Strengthened Sonko
subtitle: Faye fired his prime minister. Sonko's party holds 130 of 165 parliamentary seats. The math is not obvious.
body_md:
Senegal's President Bassirou Diomaye Faye dismissed Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko on May 22 and dissolved the government. Two days later, parliament speaker El Malick Ndiaye resigned. By May 25, Sonko's Pastef party — which commands 130 of 165 seats in the National Assembly per [RFI Afrique](https://www.rfi.fr/fr/afrique/20260524-s%C3%A9n%C3%A9gal-malick-ndiaye-annonce-sa-d%C3%A9mission-de-son-poste-de-pr%C3%A9sident-de-l-assembl%C3%A9e-nationale) — was preparing to vote on reinstating Sonko as a legislator and electing a new parliamentary speaker. The dismissal may have created the conditions for the ousted PM to become second-in-command of the state.

[AFP / France 24](https://www.france24.com/en/africa/20260524-senegal-parliament-speaker-steps-down-in-political-crisis) reported it cleanly: "Senegal's parliamentary speaker El Malick Ndiaye stepped down Sunday, clearing the way for his ally Ousmane Sonko — sacked two days ago by the country's president — to run for his position as the head of parliament." [Daily Maverick](https://www.dailymaverick.co.za/article/2026-05-25-senegals-parliament-speaker-quits-two-days-after-prime-minister-sacked/) confirmed that "members of parliament are set to convene on Tuesday to vote on reinstating Sonko as a lawmaker and to elect a new speaker for the National Assembly."

But the constitutional path is contested. [Daily Maverick](https://www.dailymaverick.co.za/article/2026-05-25-senegals-parliament-speaker-quits-two-days-after-prime-minister-sacked/) noted that critics say reinstating Sonko would be "illegal as he has never been a member of parliament." [RFI Afrique](https://www.rfi.fr/fr/afrique/20260524-s%C3%A9n%C3%A9gal-malick-ndiaye-annonce-sa-d%C3%A9mission-de-son-poste-de-pr%C3%A9sident-de-l-assembl%C3%A9e-nationale) provided the procedural sequence: Sonko must first recover his legislative seat won in the 2024 elections before any speaker vote can proceed. This eligibility dispute — foregrounded in Francophone coverage — is largely absent from Anglophone wire reporting.

The economic context for the split is stark. [AllAfrica](https://allafrica.com/stories/202605250088.html) provided the only concrete figure in the corpus: Senegal's public debt has reached the equivalent of 132% of its GDP according to the IMF. [BBC Africa](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c202jwpd24go) described the government dissolution as creating "uncertainty amid a debt crisis in the country." The Francophone wire context is more structural: [AFP / France 24 FR](https://www.france24.com/fr/vid%C3%A9o/20260524-focus-jta-s%C3%A9n%C3%A9gal-pourquoi-la-rupture-entre-sonko-et-diomaye-semblait-in%C3%A9vitable) framed the Faye-Sonko rupture as "inevitable" due to "internal rivalries, governance tensions, and economic reform divergences" — the deepest contextual reading in the corpus, treating this as a structural split rather than a presidential decision.

The corpus splits on register: Francophone outlets treat the rupture as structurally overdetermined; Anglophone wires lead with institutional uncertainty; pan-African outlets alone foreground the political energy that will shape what comes next. [AfricaNews](http://www.africanews.com/2026/05/23/sonkomania-senegals-ousted-pm-strikes-a-chord-with-disaffected-youth/) deployed "Sonkomania" as an analytical frame — treating Sonko's support base as a pan-African youth phenomenon with anti-colonial dimensions. [France 24 Spanish](https://www.france24.com/es/video/20260524-senegal-opiniones-divididas-sobre-destituci%C3%B3n-del-primer-ministro-ousmane-sonko) noted an angle absent from all English-language coverage: Sonko's dismissal could free him to run for the presidency. These forward-looking readings are not mainstream.

Two countries whose editorial judgment would be analytically significant are absent. No US outlet appears in this cluster despite Senegal's strategic importance to US Africa policy and AFRICOM engagement. No Nigerian outlet appears despite Nigeria being West Africa's largest economy and the country most likely to track a Senegalese constitutional crisis with regional implications. Their silence is not neutral: it means the English-language international record on this crisis is thin.

The dismissal as a presidential instrument to resolve a power struggle is the Anglophone frame. The dismissal as a move that strengthened the dismissed party's path to institutional control is the Francophone reading. Neither is obviously wrong. The Tuesday vote on Sonko's legislative reinstatement — and the eligibility challenge — will clarify which one better predicted the next act.
